Summary: Sole emperor from 324. Moved the imperial capital to Constantinople in 330. Issued the Edict of Milan granting religious tolerance. Presided over the Council of Nikaia in 325.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-ruled with Licinius 313-324; sons Constantinus II, Constans, and Constantius II made Caesars

Summary: Son of Constantine I. Ruled the western provinces. Killed invading his brother Constans' territory in Italy.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with brothers Constans I and Constantius II

Summary: Son of Constantine I. Ruled the Balkans and later the west. Supported Nicene Christianity. Murdered in a usurpation by Magnentius.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with brothers Constantinus II and Constantius II; sole emperor after Constantinus II's death in 340

Summary: Son of Constantine I. Sole emperor from 350. Favored Arian Christianity. Fought the Persians repeatedly.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with brothers 337-350; appointed Julian as Caesar 355

Summary: Western usurper of barbarian origin. Defeated by Constantius II at Mursa Major in 351. Committed suicide.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Usurper in the west; never recognized by Constantius II

Summary: Last non-Christian emperor. Attempted to restore traditional religion. Killed on campaign against Persia.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Caesar under Constantius II from 355; proclaimed Augustus by his troops in 360

Summary: Elected by the army after Julian's death. Made an unpopular peace with Persia ceding Nisibis. Died of uncertain causes after eight months.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor; no co-rulers

Summary: Capable military emperor who ruled the west. Defended the Rhine and Danube frontiers vigorously.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Associated his brother Valens in the east; made his son Gratian co-Augustus in 367

Summary: Eastern emperor, brother of Valentinian I. Allowed the Visigoths to settle inside the empire under poor conditions, leading to revolt. Killed at Adrianople in 378.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor in the east under arrangement with Valentinian I

Summary: Son of Valentinian I. Removed the Altar of Victory from the Roman senate. Appointed Theodosios I to rule the east. Murdered by supporters of Magnus Maximus.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-Augustus from 367; co-ruled with half-brother Valentinian II in the west

Summary: Spanish-born general who usurped power in the west. Recognized by Theodosios initially. Executed after defeat by Theodosios.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Usurper; not recognized as legitimate by eastern sources

Summary: Son of Valentinian I, nominal western co-emperor from childhood. Real power held by others. Found dead, probably murdered, at age 21.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Nominal co-emperor from infancy alongside Gratian; controlled by Arbogast in later years

Summary: Last emperor to rule a united empire. Made Nicene Christianity the state religion in 380. Defeated multiple usurpers. Divided the empire between his sons at death.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-ruled with Valentinian II and Gratian; divided empire between sons Arkadios and Honorius

Summary: Eastern emperor, son of Theodosios I. Weak ruler dominated by court factions. Oversaw expulsion of John Chrysostom.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Eastern Augustus from 383; co-emperor with father until 395

Summary: Built the Theodosian Walls. Convened the Council of Ephesos. Issued the Theodosian Law Code in 438. Largely dominated by his sister Pulcheria and senior officials.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole eastern emperor; western emperors ruled simultaneously

Summary: Military officer who married Pulcheria to gain the throne. Refused tribute to Attila. Presided over the Council of Chalkedon in 451.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole eastern emperor; western emperor Valentinian III ruled simultaneously until 455

Summary: First emperor crowned by the Patriarch of Constantinople. Broke the power of the general Aspar. Disastrous naval expedition against the Vandals in 468.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his grandson Leo II co-emperor shortly before death

Summary: Grandson of Leo I, child emperor. Co-ruled briefly with his father Zeno. Died young.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with grandfather Leo I; made his father Zeno co-emperor

Summary: Isaurian emperor. Sent Theoderic the Ostrogoth to Italy. Issued the Henotikon attempting to resolve Christological disputes, causing the Acacian Schism with Rome.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Initially co-emperor under Leo II; displaced by Basiliskos 475-476

Summary: Brother-in-law of Leo I who briefly seized power while Zeno fled. Supported Miaphysite theology. Overthrown when Zeno returned.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Usurper; made his son Marcus co-emperor

Summary: Civil servant who married the empress Ariadne. Sound financial administrator. Miaphysite sympathies caused religious unrest. Strengthened the Danube frontier.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Illiterate Macedonian peasant who rose through the army. Orthodox in religion, ending the Acacian Schism. His nephew Justinian effectively co-ruled and succeeded him.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made Justinian I co-emperor shortly before death

Summary: Reconquered North Africa, Italy, and part of Spain. Issued the Corpus Juris Civilis. Built Hagia Sophia. The Justinianic Plague devastated the empire from 541.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his cousin Justin II co-emperor near end of reign

Summary: Nephew of Justinian I. Lost most of Italy to the Lombards after 568. Wars with Persia went badly. Suffered mental breakdown; wife Sophia governed in his later years.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Adopted Tiberios as Caesar and co-ruler in 574 due to his incapacity

Summary: Popular general adopted by Justin II. Generous to the point of depleting the treasury. Fought Persia and faced Avar and Slav pressure on the Balkans.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Caesar and co-ruler from 574; sole emperor from 578

Summary: Capable military emperor. Stabilized the eastern frontier with Persia. Created the Exarchates of Ravenna and Carthage. Wrote a military manual. Executed with his sons by Phokas.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Theodosios co-emperor

Summary: Low-ranking soldier who led a mutiny against Maurikios. Brutal reign marked by purges. Lost territory to Persia. Overthrown by Herakleios.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Made Greek the official language. Dramatic comeback against Persia in the 620s recovered all lost territory. The Arab conquests then stripped away Egypt, Syria, and Mesopotamia permanently.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his sons Constantine III and Heraklonas co-emperors

Summary: Son of Herakleios. Died after only a few months, possibly poisoned by his stepmother Martina.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with father from 613; briefly co-ruled with half-brother Heraklonas

Summary: Son of Herakleios and Martina. Child emperor ruled by his mother. Both exiled and mutilated after a few months.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor with half-brother Constantine III; mother Martina held real power

Summary: Fought the Arab advance with limited success. Moved to Syracuse, the only emperor to abandon Constantinople. Murdered in his bath.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his sons Constantine IV, Herakleios, and Tiberios co-emperors

Summary: Repelled the first Arab siege of Constantinople 674-678 using Greek fire. Convened the Third Council of Constantinople. Forced recognition of Bulgaria.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Constans II; forced to demote brothers Herakleios and Tiberios as co-emperors

Summary: Deposed in 695 with his nose cut off and sent into exile. Returned with Bulgarian and Khazar support in 705. Second reign marked by vengeance and erratic behavior. Murdered in a military revolt. Both reigns combined into one row.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor in first reign; made his son Tiberios co-emperor in second reign

Summary: General who overthrew Justinian II. Himself deposed by Tiberios III. Later executed when Justinian II returned.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole ruler; usurper

Summary: Naval officer who overthrew Leontios. Reasonably competent reign. Executed when Justinian II returned.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole ruler; usurper

Summary: Armenian general who overthrew Justinian II. Monothelite in theology. Overthrown by his own troops.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole ruler; usurper

Summary: Capable administrator who attempted to reform the army. Deposed by military revolt. Later attempted a comeback and was executed.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Tax official elevated by a mutinous army. Abdicated voluntarily in favor of Leo III.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Defended Constantinople against the second great Arab siege of 717-718. Initiated the iconoclast controversy from 726. Reorganized the theme system.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Constantine V co-emperor in 720

Summary: Vigorous military emperor and committed iconoclast. Convened the iconoclast Council of Hiereia in 754. Won significant victories against Bulgaria and the Arabs. Deeply reviled by later pro-icon sources, making his reign hard to evaluate fairly.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Leo III from 720; displaced briefly by usurper Artabasdos 741-743; made his son Leo IV co-emperor

Summary: Son-in-law of Leo III who briefly seized power during Constantine V's absence. Pro-icon. Defeated and blinded by Constantine V.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Usurper; made his sons co-emperors

Summary: Son of Constantine V. Moderate iconoclast. Died young, leaving his widow Eirene as regent for Constantine VI.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Constantine V; made his son Constantine VI co-emperor

Summary: Ruled under his mother Eirene's regency, then in his own right. Blinded and deposed by his own mother.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Leo IV; ruled jointly with Eirene until he asserted sole rule

Summary: First woman to rule the empire in her own name. Restored icon veneration. Deposed by her own finance minister.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Had been regent and co-empress for Constantine VI before deposing him; ruled alone

Summary: Finance minister who overthrew Eirene. Capable administrator. Killed fighting the Bulgarians at Pliska in 811, the first emperor to die in battle against a foreign enemy since Valens.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Staurakios co-emperor

Summary: Son of Nikephoros I, wounded in the same battle as his father. Died of his wounds after a few months.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Nikephoros I

Summary: Brother-in-law of Staurakios. Pious and ineffective. Lost a major battle to Bulgaria and abdicated, becoming a monk.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his sons co-emperors

Summary: General who overthrew Michael I. Revived iconoclasm. Murdered in the palace chapel on Christmas Day.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Symbatios co-emperor

Summary: Low-born soldier who murdered Leo V. Founded the Amorian dynasty. Moderate iconoclast. Faced the serious revolt of Thomas the Slav.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Theophilos co-emperor

Summary: Cultivated, architecturally ambitious emperor. Last iconoclast emperor. Fought the Arabs with mixed results.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Michael II; made his son Michael III co-emperor

Summary: Last of the Amorian dynasty. Child emperor under his mother Theodora's regency. Icon veneration permanently restored in 843. Christianization of the Slavs began under his reign. Murdered by Basil I.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made Basil I co-emperor in 866

Summary: Macedonian peasant who rose to become co-emperor and murdered Michael III. Founded the Macedonian dynasty. Capable military and legislative emperor.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Michael III 866-867; made sons Konstantinos and Leo VI co-emperors

Summary: Prolific legislator who completed the Basilika law code. Fourth marriage caused a major church crisis. Military reverses against Bulgaria and the Arabs.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Basil I from 870; made his brother Alexander co-emperor

Summary: Brother of Leo VI. Brief sole reign. Repudiated peace with Bulgaria, leading to devastating war. Died of illness.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Leo VI for decades; brief sole reign

Summary: Long reign mostly spent under regencies and the domination of Romanos I Lekapenos. A patron of learning who wrote important administrative and ceremonial texts.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Nominal emperor from infancy; dominated by Romanos I Lekapenos 920-944; made his son Romanos II co-emperor

Summary: Admiral who married his daughter to Constantine VII and made himself senior emperor. Issued legislation protecting small farmers. Deposed by his own sons.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Senior co-emperor alongside the legitimate Constantine VII; made his sons co-emperors

Summary: Son of Constantine VII. Short reign. The general Nikephoros Phokas won major victories against the Arabs. Died young under suspicious circumstances.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Constantine VII from 945; made his sons Basil II and Constantine VIII co-emperors

Summary: Great general who conquered Cilicia and parts of Syria. Married the empress-widow Theophano. Murdered by his nephew John Tzimiskes.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled alongside the child co-emperors Basil II and Constantine VIII

Summary: Brilliant military emperor who murdered Nikephoros II. Pushed deep into Syria and Palestine. Died of illness, possibly poisoned.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled alongside the child co-emperors Basil II and Constantine VIII

Summary: Longest-reigning Byzantine emperor. Destroyed Bulgaria in a long war, blinding 15,000 prisoners. Christianized Kievan Rus through the baptism of Vladimir I. Left the treasury overflowing.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor from infancy under Nikephoros II and John I Tzimiskes; ruled alongside brother Constantine VIII throughout

Summary: Brother of Basil II, nominal co-emperor for decades. Sole reign was short and ineffective. Married his daughter Zoe to a series of emperors.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Basil II for nearly 50 years; brief sole reign

Summary: Prefect of Constantinople who married the empress Zoe. Pretentious and militarily incompetent. Found drowned in his bath, almost certainly murdered.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled jointly with empress Zoe

Summary: Young man of humble origin who caught empress Zoe's attention. Real power held by his brother John the Orphanotrophos. Suffered from epilepsy.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled jointly with empress Zoe; his nephew Michael V was made co-emperor near end of reign

Summary: Nephew of Michael IV, adopted by Zoe. Attempted to exile Zoe and rule alone, triggering a popular revolt. Blinded and deposed.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Initially co-emperor with empress Zoe; attempted sole rule

Summary: Daughter of Constantine VIII. Briefly ruled jointly with her sister Theodora after the people of Constantinople overthrew Michael V.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled jointly with sister Theodora

Summary: Daughter of Constantine VIII. Ruled jointly with Zoe briefly, then as sole empress after Zoe's marriage to Constantine IX, and finally alone after his death. Last of the Macedonian dynasty.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-ruled with Zoe in 1042; otherwise sole empress

Summary: Married empress Zoe to gain the throne. Ineffective ruler who reduced the military budget. The Great Schism between Rome and Constantinople occurred in 1054.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled jointly with empress Zoe until her death in 1050, then alongside Theodora

Summary: Elderly official chosen by Theodora's court. Unable to manage the military aristocracy. Abdicated after Isaac Komnenos raised a revolt.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: First of the Komnenian family to rule. Energetic reformer who tried to restore financial and military order. Abdicated voluntarily, becoming a monk.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Civilian bureaucrat. Reduced military spending further. The Seljuk Turks began raiding Anatolia under his reign.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his sons Michael VII and Konstantios co-emperors

Summary: Military emperor who married the empress-dowager Eudokia. Defeated and captured by the Seljuks at Mantzikert in 1071, the catastrophic turning point that opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled alongside the Doukas co-emperors Michael VII, Konstantios, and Andronikos

Summary: Son of Constantine X. Scholarly but ineffective. His reign saw the loss of most of Anatolia to the Seljuks. Abdicated and became a monk.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Constantine X and Romanos IV; made his son Constantine co-emperor

Summary: Elderly general who seized the throne. Unable to manage multiple revolts. Abdicated when Alexios Komnenos marched on Constantinople.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled alongside the child co-emperor Constantine Doukas

Summary: Founded the Komnenian system of government based on family networks. Appealed to the west for help, triggering the First Crusade. Gradually recovered western Anatolia.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son John II co-emperor

Summary: Considered by many the best of the Komnenian emperors. Vigorous campaigns in Anatolia, the Balkans, and Syria. Died in a hunting accident.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Alexios I from 1092; made his son Manuel I co-emperor

Summary: Ambitious western-oriented emperor who intervened heavily in Italian and crusader politics. Devastating defeat by the Seljuks at Myriokephalon in 1176.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under John II; made his son Alexios II co-emperor

Summary: Son of Manuel I. Child emperor under his mother's regency. Strangled on the orders of his cousin Andronikos I.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Manuel I; mother Maria of Antioch acted as regent

Summary: Last of the Komnenian dynasty. Charismatic adventurer who seized power. Attempted radical administrative reform. Lynched by a Constantinople mob when the Normans invaded.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Initially co-emperor with Alexios II before having him killed

Summary: Founded the Angelid dynasty. Defeated the Norman invasion. Failed to suppress the Bulgarian revolt that created the Second Bulgarian Empire. Deposed and blinded by his brother Alexios III.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Alexios IV co-emperor

Summary: Brother who overthrew Isaac II. Ineffective ruler who presided over the empire's accelerating decline. Fled when the Fourth Crusade appeared before Constantinople.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor

Summary: Restored by the Fourth Crusade along with his son Alexios IV. Blind and incapable. Killed when Alexios V seized power.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-ruled with son Alexios IV

Summary: Son of Isaac II who invited the Fourth Crusade to restore his father. Unable to pay the crusaders what he had promised. Strangled by Alexios V.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-ruled with his father Isaac II

Summary: Seized power promising to defend the city against the crusaders. Constantinople fell to the Fourth Crusade in April 1204. Fled and was later executed.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor; usurper

Summary: Founded the Empire of Nikaia after the Latin conquest of Constantinople. Fought the Latins, Bulgarians, and Seljuks to establish a viable rump state. Crowned emperor in 1208.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor of Nikaia

Summary: Greatest emperor of the Nikaia period. Expanded the empire significantly. Sound economic administration. Venerated as a saint in the Orthodox church.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Made his son Theodore II co-emperor

Summary: Son of John III. Intellectual emperor who wrote theological and scientific works. Suspicious of the aristocracy. Died of epilepsy.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under John III; made his son John IV co-emperor

Summary: Child emperor, son of Theodore II. Blinded and imprisoned by Michael VIII Palaiologos after the recovery of Constantinople.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor alongside Michael VIII from 1259

Summary: Recovered Constantinople from the Latins in 1261. Founded the Palaiologan dynasty. Attempted union with Rome to ward off western threats. The Sicilian Vespers of 1282 relieved the western threat. Blinded the legitimate heir John IV Laskaris to secure his own dynasty.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Initially co-emperor with John IV Laskaris, whom he blinded; made his son Andronikos II co-emperor

Summary: Long reign marked by financial difficulty, the disastrous hiring of the Catalan Company, and civil war with his grandson Andronikos III. The Ottoman emirate consolidated during his reign.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Michael VIII from 1261; made his son Michael IX and later grandson Andronikos III co-emperors

Summary: Co-emperor son of Andronikos II. Never ruled alone. Military setbacks against the Bulgarians and Turks. Died of grief after his son accidentally caused the death of another of his sons.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor only; never sole ruler

Summary: Overthrew his grandfather Andronikos II after civil war. Energetic but faced relentless Ottoman expansion. Last emperor to lead a significant military campaign in Anatolia.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Andronikos II from 1316; made his son John V co-emperor

Summary: Longest-reigning Palaiologan emperor though interrupted by usurpers. Devastating civil war of 1341-1347. The Black Death and inexorable Ottoman advance defined his reign. Personally converted to Roman Catholicism.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Andronikos III; dominated by John VI Kantakouzenos 1347-1354; displaced by his son Andronikos IV 1376-1379 and briefly by grandson John VII in 1390

Summary: Aristocrat and general who won the civil war against John V's regents. Fatally invited Ottoman forces into Europe as allies. Abdicated and became a monk and historian.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Ruled as senior co-emperor alongside the legitimate John V

Summary: Son of John V who rebelled against his father with Ottoman support. Briefly held Constantinople. Reconciled and made co-emperor.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Usurper against his father John V; later recognized as co-emperor

Summary: Grandson of John V who briefly seized Constantinople with Ottoman support. Reconciled with the family. Later served as regent and governor of Thessalonike.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Brief usurper; later legitimate co-emperor and regent

Summary: Cultivated and able emperor facing an impossible situation. Survived the Ottoman siege of 1394-1402. Timur's defeat of the Ottomans at Ankara in 1402 gave the empire a reprieve. A noted writer.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under John V from 1373; made his son John VIII co-emperor

Summary: Son of Manuel II. Attended the Council of Florence in 1439 and agreed to union with Rome in exchange for promised western aid. The union was deeply unpopular at home and the crusade was destroyed at Varna in 1444.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Co-emperor under Manuel II from 1421; made his brother Constantine XI co-emperor near death

Summary: Last Byzantine emperor. Defended Constantinople with inadequate forces against Mehmed II. Died fighting when the walls were breached on 29 May 1453. Became a legendary figure in Greek folk memory.
Co_Emperor_Notes: Sole emperor at the end; no co-emperor
